localizedDescriptions.en: An assessment scale used to screen for frailty and to broadly stratify degrees of fitness and frailty in an older adult.
Default, unspecified point in time event which may be explicitly defined in a template or at run-time.
Assessed level of frailty.
People who are robust, active, energetic and motivated. These people commonly exercise regularly. They are among the fittest for their age.
People who have no active disease symptoms but are less fit than category 1. Often, they exercise or are very active occasionally, e.g. seasonally.
People whose medical problems are well controlled, but are not regularly active beyond routine walking.
While not dependent on others for daily help, often symptoms limit activities. A common complaint is being "slowed up", and/or being tired during the day.

These people often have more evident slowing, and need help in high order IADLs (finances, transportation, heavy housework, medications). Typically, mild frailty progressively impairs shopping and walking outside alone, meal preparation and housework.

People need help with all outside activities and with keeping house. Inside, they often have problems with stairs and need help with bathing and might need minimal assistance (cuing, standby) with dressing.
Completely dependent for personal care, from whatever cause (physical or cognitive). Even so, they seem stable and not at high risk of dying (within - 6 monts).
Completely dependent, approaching the end of life. Typically, they could not recover even from a minor illness.
Approaching the end of life. This category applies to people with a life expectancy <6 months, who are not otherwise evidently frail.

annotations.comment: Also known as the Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale.

localizedDescriptions.en: Category representing the overall severity of the symptom or sign.

annotations.comment: Defining values such as mild, moderate or severe in such a way that is applicable to multiple symptoms or signs plus allows multiple users to interpret and record them consistently is not easy. Some organisations extend the value set further with inclusion of additional values such as 'Trivial' and 'Very severe', and/or 'Mild-Moderate' and 'Moderate-Severe', adds to the definitional difficulty and may also worsen inter-recorder reliability issues. Use of 'Life-threatening' and 'Fatal' is also often considered as part of this value set, although from a pure point of view it may actually reflect an outcome rather than a severity. In view of the above, keeping to a well-defined but smaller list is preferred and so the mild/moderate/severe value set is offered, however the choice of other text allows for other value sets to be included at this data element in a template. Note: more specific grading of severity can be recorded using the 'Specific details' SLOT.

localizedDescriptions.en: A scoring system to grade and follow the development of organ dysfunction in six vital organ systems. Previously known as "Sepsis related Organ Failure Assessment".
Default, unspecified point in time or interval event which may be explicitly defined in a template or at run-time.

The mean arterial pressure (MAP), or the need for vasopressors (VP), (dopamine (DA), adrenaline (A), noradrenaline (NA) or dobutamine) are indicators for a possible dysfunction of the cardiovascular system.
The mean arterial pressure (MAP) is greater than or equal to 70 mmHg.
The mean arterial pressure (MAP) is less than 70 mmHg.
The dopamine (DA) dosage is less than 5 µg/kg/minute, or any dosage of dobutamine.

The dosage of dopamine (DA) is greater than 5 µg/kg/minute, or the dosage of noradrenaline (NA) is less than or equal to 0.1 µg/kg/minute, or the dosage of adrenaline (A) is less than or equal to 0.1 µg/kg/minutt.
The dosage of noradrenaline (N) or adrenaline (A) is greater than 0.1 µg/kg/minute.
The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is an indicator for a possible dysfunction of the central nervous system.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 13 or 14.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 10, 11 or 12.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 6, 7, 8 or 9.
Glasgow Coma Scale is less than 6.
Creatinine concentration and 24 h urine output (UOP) are indicators for a possible dysfunction of the central nervous system.
The creatinine concentration is less than 1.2 mg/dL. Equivalent to creatinine < 110 µmol/L.
The creatinine concentration is between 1.2 and 1.9 mg/dL. Equivalent to creatinine 110 - 170 µmol/L.
The creatinine concentration is between 2.0 and 3.4 mg/dL. Equivalent to creatinine 171 - 299 µmol/L.
The creatinine concentration is between 3.5 and 4.9 mg/dL, or the urine output is between 500 and 200 mL/24h. Equivalent to creatinine 300 - 440 µmol/L or UOP < 500 mL/24h.
The creatinine concentration is greater than or equal to 5.0 mg/dL, or the urine output is less than 200 mL/24h. Equivalent to creatinine > 440 µmol/L or UOP < 200 mL/24h.

annotations.comment: In some localities, creatinine concentration is measured in μmol/L.

localizedDescriptions.en: Bilirubin concentration is an indicator for a possible dysfunction of the central nervous system.
The bilirubin concentration is less than 1.2 mg/dL. Equivalent to bilirubin < 20 µmol/L.
The bilirubin concentration is between 1.2 and 1.9 mg/dL. Equivalent to bilirubin 20-32 µmol/L.
The bilirubin concentration is between 2.0 and 5.9 mg/dL. Equivalent to bilirubin 33-101 µmol/L.
The bilirubin concentration is between 6.0 and 11.9 mg/dL. Equivalent to bilirubin 102-204 µmol/L.
The bilirubin concentration is greater than or equal to 12.0 mg/dL. Equivalent to bilirubin > 204 µmol/L.

annotations.comment: In some localities, bilirubin concentration is measured in μmol/L.

localizedDescriptions.en: Platelets concentration is an indicator for a possible dysfunction of the blood clotting system.
The platelet concentration is greater than or equal to 150,000 /µL.
The platelet concentration is between 150,000 and 100,000 /µL.
The platelet concentration is between 100,000 and 50,000 /µL.
The platelet concentration is between 50,000 and 20,000 /µL.
The platelet concentration is less than 20,000 /µL.
The total sum of each component parameter for the SOFA score.
The characteristics of spontaneous breathing by an individual.
Default, unspecified point in time or interval event which may be explicitly defined in a template or at run-time.
The frequency of spontaneous breathing.
The local measurement of arterial blood pressure which is a surrogate for arterial pressure in the systemic circulation.
Default, unspecified point in time or interval event which may be explicitly defined in a template or at run-time.
Peak systemic arterial blood pressure  - measured in systolic or contraction phase of the heart cycle.
Minimum systemic arterial blood pressure - measured in the diastolic or relaxation phase of the heart cycle.

annotations.comment: Most commonly, use of the term 'blood pressure' refers to measurement of brachial artery pressure in the upper arm.

The rate of the pulse or heart beat, measured in beats per minute.
